(ATMS) Assessment Report for National

Sounder Science Investigator-led Processing 
System (SIPS) Data Level 1 

August 2021

Software Version v03_15 

Author: Mathias Schreier 

Jet Propulsion Laboratory, California Institute of Technology, USA

This report was carried out at the Jet Propulsion Laboratory, California Institute of Technology, 
under a contract with the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (80NM0018D0004). 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 JPSS-1 ATMS Assessment Report for SNPP Sounder SIPS Level 1B

2 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 JPSS-1 ATMS Assessment Report for SNPP Sounder SIPS Level 1B 

1. Introduction 

This document describes the assessment of Version 3 of SNPP/JPSS-1 ATMS Level 1B 
(L1B) Brightness Temperature V3 product (product names: SNDRJ1ATMSL1B V3 and 
SNPPATMSL1B V3), available at NASA GDAAC. These products are the official 
NASA SNPP/JPSS-1 ATMS products processed with algorithms developed by the 
SNPP/JPSS Sounder SIPS at Jet Propulsion Lab (JPL) and produced by the Sounder SIPS 
at GES DISC.  Version 3 products created and released in 2021 are based on Version 3 of 
the SNPP ATMS algorithms. Version 3 includes all software fixes since the release of 
SNPP Version 1 and also features new channel-dependent error characteristic for lunar 
intrusions into space calibration scenes. 

The purpose of this document is to give a short assessment of the Version 3 ATMS data 
from SNPP and JPSS-1. Version 3 is different from Version 2 in a few aspects, because 
corrections were applied in Version 3. The main corrections are: 

1.  Correction of the multiplication coefficients for shelf-temperature calculations 
2.  Correction of the sun glint calculation 
3.  Additional variables and changes in the variable order 

The first correction is affecting the observed brightness temperatures from the calibration 
and we will briefly discuss the reasons and the resulting impact on the calibrated 
brightness temperatures.  

The focus of this report is on comparisons with V2, to explain the impacts of the 
corrections between the new and the old version.  

Additionally, we will also provide some comparisons of the data processed by NOAA 
using the Interface Data Processing Segment (IDPS), called IDPS TDR (Temperature 
Data Record) from here on, and available at the NOAA Comprehensive Large Array-data 
Stewardship System (CLASS).  The reasoning for this comparison with the IDPS TDR is 
based on the fact, that NOAA and NASA have different approaches of correcting and 
reprocessing their data: 

The main NOAA focus is weather prediction and therefore data reprocessing is not a 
priority. Once an update is made to the IDPS algorithm, the forward processing stream 
continues without retrospective changes to the record. The NASA focus is on continuity 
and reliability of long-term records. Maintenance and reprocessing of the NASA record 
are therefore mandatory. As an example: after an update of the calibration coefficients for 
SNPP, all data since launch was reprocessed. This resulted in a Version 2 record, which 
replaced Version 1 in March 2018. The majority of the code for SNPPATMSL1B V2 
generation is also used for the current processing of SNDRJ1ATMSL1B V2. This 
ensures the continuity of the ATMS data over both platforms and includes retroactive 
improvements based on Version 2. The same is the case now here for this Version 3: The 
entire NASA data record was reprocessed and is replacing Version 2. This is to avoid 
jumps and discontinuity in the dataset.  

3 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 JPSS-1 ATMS Assessment Report for SNPP Sounder SIPS Level 1B 

Differences between NOAA and NASA datasets can therefore be confusing to the user: 
For example, the NOAA IDPS processing code for ATMS SNPP was changed 
significantly in March 2017. This included an update of the calibration coefficients, but it 
was done without any reprocessing of data before March 2017.  SIPS did similar 
improvements, but the entire dataset since launch in 2011 was reprocessed, resulting in 
SNPPATMSL1B V2, and now V3. The NASA SNPPATMSL1B V2 is therefore a 
product with better continuity and we consider it the better option for long-term studies. 

Overall, both the Sounder SIPS products and also the IDPS TDR are in a very good state 
now. Differences between the products are now mainly because of different approaches 
in the calibration that will be discussed later. Especially the calibrated brightness 
temperatures from ATMS on JPSS-1 are already in a very good state, since JPSS-1 
benefits from the SNPP testing phase, and both NASA and NOAA started with improved 
algorithms based on the SNPP legacy. Also, to our knowledge, there have been no 
significant changes to NOAA IDPS processing routines since JPSS-1 launch in 
November 2017. Therefore, current ATMS TDR brightness temperature data from JPSS-
1 are processed with the same code since launch in November 2017.  

Another reason that distinguishes the datasets SNDRJ1ATMSL1B V3 and 
SNPPATMSL1B V3 from the IDPS TDR is the consistency:  
Apart from the reprocessing of the entire mission, the data from SNPPATMSL1B V3 and 
SNDRJ1ATMSL1B V3 are also based on “identical” processing. Every aspect of 
improvement that was found for the JPSS-1 version was also applied to the SNPP 
version, if applicable. And the same was applied vice versa. The reprocessing of ATMS 
SNPP and JPSS-1 ATMS brightness temperatures with the same code ensures a 
continuous and seamless ATMS data set since the start of SNPP in 2011: 
SNPPATMSL1B V3 was reprocessed for the entire mission of SNPP since launch in 
2011. SNDRJ1ATMSL1B V3 was reprocessed for the entire mission since launch in 
2017. This data set is therefore suitable for inter-instrument comparisons, and also 
consistent with the NASA need for long-term, consistent records from multiple sensors. 

1.1 The NASA Approach for ATMS L1 

A goal of NASA Earth observations is highly reliable long-term satellite geophysical data 
records with well-characterized uncertainties and reliable and reproducible error 
corrections. For L1B, this requires control over processing steps like calibration, error 
characterization and product maintenance. The development of NOAA products, 
however, is focused on short-term operational applications. NOAA products are therefore 
less useful in terms of long-term stability or the retrospective error corrections important 
for climate research applications. For this and other reasons NASA decided to create its 
own, independent ATMS Level 1 product, which can be created, updated and maintained 
separately from the NOAA IDPS TDR product. 

The creation of a parallel and independent algorithm and product gives several 
advantages: 

4 

 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 JPSS-1 ATMS Assessment Report for SNPP Sounder SIPS Level 1B 

•  More control over calibration procedures and uncertainties. 
•  More control of consistency and continuity, both with older datasets, but also with 
future microwave products, like those from current and planned JPSS satellites. 
•  The option for addendums of important information in the data files, like moon 

angle or land fraction. 

•  The creation of a Level 1A dataset that includes radiometric counts before 

calibration, which is helpful for error and calibration analysis. 

Similar to the original NOAA product, the NASA product is based on the binary 
telemetric packets from Suomi NPP and is created almost in real-time. However, the 
NASA conversion and calibration algorithms were developed and maintained by the 
SNPP Sounding science team in collaboration with the SNPP Sounder SIPS at JPL and 
maintained independently from the NOAA IDPS algorithms. 

The difference in the products with respect to brightness temperatures is small: NASA 
and NOAA follow the same basic calibration procedure for microwave instruments. A 
detailed description of the current NASA calibration procedure and conversion can be 
found in the ATMS ATBD [1].  However, there is a major difference NASA and NOAA 
calibration, which is an effect of changes in the IDPS code in 2017: the NOAA IDPS 
code converts from counts to radiance units. Corrections like quadratic correction are 
therefore all converted into radiances, and  brightness temperatures are calculated at the 
end of the conversion. The NASA approach has not changed from the brightness 
temperature calibration. This  approach was used for NASA instruments like AMSU-A 
on Aqua and ATMS on SNPP. Also, calibration values from the manufacturer are usually 
given in temperatures. These differences between NASA and NOAA data has slight 
implications for their comparison, as will be shown in the assessment section below. 

The initial goal of SNPPATMSL1B V1 was to create similar brightness temperatures as 
IDPS TDR and minimize the deviation between the two products, as proof of correct 
implementation.   However, the goal for Version 2 and subsequent versions of the NASA 
L1B algorithm, and also future JPSS instruments, is to implement improvements and – if 
necessary, to re-process the data. This means the re-processing will be applied to all 
affected ATMS instruments from SNPP to all future JPSS platforms, to ensure continuity 
of the data. NOAA has no plans for re-processing, so this could lead to significant 
retroactive differences and cause substantial difference between the two product streams. 

SNDRJ1ATMSL1B and SNPPATMSL1B deviate from IDPS TDR also with respect to 
format and variables:  

•  The NASA product is created in netCDF.  
•  The file granule size is different from NOAA’s and similar to other NASA 

• 

products.  
It includes a variety of additional variables from both L0 and L1, such as moon 
angle and error characteristics. This allows better data filtering, error estimates 
and also conformity with other NASA microwave products. 

5 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 JPSS-1 ATMS Assessment Report for SNPP Sounder SIPS Level 1B 

1.2 Changes in NASA L1 Version 2 

Changes and improvements for Version 2 are described in detail in the assessment reports 
for Version 2. For comprehensiveness, we just give a short overview here. 

V2 has two major improvements compared to the Version 1: 

•  Errors in the calibration coefficients were found in 2017, which were affecting 
both the NASA and the NOAA IDPS algorithms.  These were corrected. 
•  NASA is producing an error flag indicating lunar contamination of the space 

view. Version 1 had a single threshold of 4 degrees for all channels. An analysis 
of the data found this insufficient and a channel dependent approach was 
developed instead. 

The lunar contamination flag is only a small change compared to Version 1, and it is 
mainly improving the error flagging of lunar intrusions.  The error in calibration 
coefficients has a bigger impact and the impacts can be seen in the assessment report for 
SNPP. However, it should be emphasized that  it is necessary to understand the timeline 
of changes in the IDPS code to understand the differences between the NOAA and 
NASA data.  This is especially so for the changes in the NOAA calibration procedure of 
ATMS: it is now done with a radiance conversion, not with brightness temperatures. 
NASA decided not to follow this path and to calibrate in brightness temperature space 
until NOAA’s IDPS change has been fully evaluated. The IDPS change therefore results 
in a deviation, which will be discussed in the assessment section. The discussion about 
advantages and disadvantages of calibration in brightness temperature or radiance space 
is not part of this assessment. It is sufficient to understand that it will need to be assessed. 

1.3 Changes in NASA L1 Version 3 

This report is mainly focusing on changes for Version 3. Compared to Version 2, the 
changes for Version 3 are minor, but still important for certain user needs: 

•  A mistake in the shelf-temperature calculation was found. The mistake was 

corrected and compared with the former values. The differences were usually 
around < 1K. However, the shelf-temperature is part of the calibration process, so 
differences in observed brightness temperatures were assessed  

•  A mistake in the sun glint calculation was found. The mistake resulted in 

deviations of up to 25 degrees from the correct sun glint angle. The value is not 
needed in the calibration or any L1B calculation, so it did not affect other 
products. 

•  Geospatial bounds are declared now with longitude first, consistent with other 

NASA products 

•  The label “units” was removed from unitless netcdf-variables 

6 

 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 JPSS-1 ATMS Assessment Report for SNPP Sounder SIPS Level 1B 

2.  Assessment 

The differences between Version 1 and Version 2, as well as differences between Version 
2 and TDR IDPS, are discussed in much detail in the old assessment report for Version 2, 
especially the one for SNPPATMSL1B V2. So, to understand detailed differences 
between NOAA and NASA datasets, we refer to the assessment report for Version 2, 
especially SNPPATMSL1B V2. 

The new report at hand will therefore focus on the assessment on the Sounder SIPS 
Version 3.  It is focusing on both datasets for ATMS, the data from SNPP and from 
JPSS-1, and will focus on possible differences in comparison to Version 2 and only touch 
a few comparisons with IDPS TDR.  

This assessment will focus on 2 points: correction of shelf temperature calculation and 
correction of sun glint calculation. 

•  We tested the impact of the shelf temperature correction on the system. To 

achieve this, we looked first on the direct impact of the correction on the shelf 
temperature itself. Additionally, we estimated any impacts of this correction on 
the calibration and the brightness temperatures. 

•  Then we looked into the sun glint angle correction. This correction has no impact 

on the brightness temperatures, but is a useful variable for users who are 
interested in possible impacts of the sun glint on the observation. 

Additionally, we retested the data in comparison to IDPS TDR data. This similar to the 
work done for Version 2 and therefore more a consistency check. 

2.1 Correction of the Shelf Temperature Calculation: Comparison between V2 
and V3 

With respect to updates from Version 2 to Version 3, we first assessed the impact of the 
shelf temperature correction. The direct impact of the correction can only be seen in the 
L1A product, not in L1B, because only the L1A product includes the variable shelf_prt. 
The temperature is calculated for each band with different coefficients, resulting therefore 
in 4 values per observation. The difference per band for random sample day is shown in 
Figure 1.  

7 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 JPSS-1 ATMS Assessment Report for SNPP Sounder SIPS Level 1B 

Figure 1: Shelf temperature difference after the correction of the coefficients for a 
sample of data on day 20202/06/01 for ATMS on SNPP (left) JPSS-1 (right) 

The correction has a stronger impact on the SNPP data than on the JPSS-1 data. For 
SNPP, we find differences of ~0.2 and -1.7 K. For JPSS-1, the correction results only in a 
change of 0.04 K. The impact on the shelf temperature is therefore small, especially for 
JPSS-1.  

However, the shelf temperature is part of the calibration, because it is part of the non-
linearity correction (see ATBD for details). The impact is non-linear and different for 
every channel. It is therefore difficult to estimate from a single observation point 
difference. We sampled therefore a variety of data points from a random day and looked 
on the statistical impact of the shelf temperature on the brightness temperature. 

The results are shown in Figure 2. 

Figure 2: Brightness temperature difference after the correction of the coefficients for 
a sample of data on day 20202/06/01 for ATMS on SNPP (left) JPSS-1 (right). Blue is 
mean, grey is standard deviation. 

8 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 JPSS-1 ATMS Assessment Report for SNPP Sounder SIPS Level 1B 

It is very obvious, that the correction has a minor impact  on the brightness temperatures. 
For SNPP, the impact is less than 0.003 K and mainly impacting the channels for water 
vapor (19-22). Channel 1 and 17 show the strongest variability, but the range is only in 
the range of 0.001K.  

For JPSS-1, the differences are even smaller. Please observe the scaling factor of 1E-5, 
indicating that none of the variations is higher than 8E-4 degrees and therefore lower than 
the noise estimate of the instrument, which varies from 0.1 to 0.5 K, dependent on the 
channel.  

Nevertheless, we found it important to apply the correction and to make users aware of 
possible differences in brightness temperatures, when switching from Version 2 to 
Version 3. 

2.2 Correction of the Sun Glint Calculation: Comparison between V2 and V3 

Sun glint is mainly interesting for visible or near-infrared observations. It can, however, 
be interesting into the impact of microwave sounders, especially when users look into int 
in comparison to other instruments with different wavelengths. The variables 
sun_glint_lat, sun_glint_lon, and sun_glint_dist  are therefore part of the output-file. A 
mistake in Version 2 was detected and the sun glint calculation was corrected for Version 
3. 

9 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 JPSS-1 ATMS Assessment Report for SNPP Sounder SIPS Level 1B 

Figure 3: Distance from center of sun glint before (upper row) and after the correction 
(lower row) for a sample of data on day 20202/06/01 for ATMS on SNPP (left) JPSS-1 
(right). Night values and values above 1000 km are omitted. 

To assess this impact, we used a sample of sun glint calculations and compared the 
results. Figure 3 shows the impact of the correction. 

The Figure shows a comparison of the old Version 2 in the upper row and the corrected 
new Version 3 in the lower row. In the old version, the center of the sun glint was placed 
over Africa for SNPP and over South Pacific for JPSS-1. The corrected version in the 
lower row shows both centers in the Mediterranean area. The location is corrected as well 
as the distances. The corrected Version 3 makes also more sense, because SNPP and 
JPSS-1 have currently a difference in overflight time of ~15-30 min and the closer sun 
glint calculations in Version 3 are therefore much more reasonable. 

2.2 Comparison of Version 3 and IDPS TDR : Statistical Comparison of Collocated 
Brightness Temperatures 

With respect to IDPS TDR, we do only a short discussion about the differences between 
V3 and IDPS TDR. These differences are not deviating much from the comparisons of 
Version 2 and the IDPS TDR, because the main rift between the two calibration systems 
occurred already in 2017 during the development of Version 2:   
The big difference between NASA and NOAA processing for ATMS is the conversion of 
counts to brightness temperatures. NOAA developed a process to do this in radiance 
units, which mandates a conversion of all correction factors, like the quadratic correction, 

10 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 JPSS-1 ATMS Assessment Report for SNPP Sounder SIPS Level 1B 

into radiance units. The NOAA IDPS processing was switched to this system during the 
SNPP mission, before the JPSS-1 launch.   

NASA does not follow this approach but will consider it for future versions. The NASA 
conversion is still done in brightness temperature units and any correction is applied in 
those units. Correction factors were directly extracted from the calibration data book [2]. 
The values are in temperatures and therefore no conversion is necessary in this case.  
The impact is nearly identical for SNPP and JPSS-1 and are described in detail in the 
assessment reports for Version 2.  

For this report, we just do a repeat of these tests for Version 3 (JPSS-1), to verify the 
consistency in the results. 

2.3 Statistical Comparison of Collocated Brightness Temperatures 

The following paragraphs will focus on the statistical difference between collocated IDPS 
and NASA data, using data from ATMS on JPSS-1. Figure 4 shows the difference in 
Sounder SIPS ATMS-V3 and IDPS for January 1st , 2020. The results are shown for 
Channel 5 and 16; further channels are available in the Addendum. As can be seen, 
differences are all of order 0.01 K and close to Gaussian. There is also no significant 
change between February and November. The variation very similar to comparisons of 
SIPS ATMS Version 2 and based on the effect of differences in calibration calculation: 
IDPS is calculating in radiance units since 2017, but there are also small difference in 
averaging of the scan lines. The details are explained in the assessment report for Version 
2. Here it is sufficient to say that most channels are similar to the distribution for channel 
5 and that the difference between IDPS and NASA processing is small. An exception is 
channel 15, a channel with surface influence: Here the variation can be higher, which is 
significantly higher than the other channels.  

Figure 4: Brightness temperature differences of Sounder SIPS ATMS-V3 and IDPS 
for channel 5 and 16 June 1st, 2020. 

2.4 Scene Difference 

One of the minor differences between NOAA and NASA algorithms is the choice of 
calibration weighting functions. The details are described in the ATMS ATBD [1], so 

11 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 JPSS-1 ATMS Assessment Report for SNPP Sounder SIPS Level 1B 

here we will only describe the principle:  to calibrate a scan line, the hot and cold target-
counts are each averaged over several scan lines. The averaging is dependent on a 
weighting function. NASA and NOAA use slightly different weighting functions: IDPS 
applies a channel dependent box function, averaging 5-10 scan lines. Some changes were 
introduced during the mission, mainly during the beginning, to avoid “striping”. NASA is 
also using a channel dependent box function between 5 and 9 scans, but uses the same 
function for the entire mission timeline. 

The differences between IDPS and Sounder SIPS ATMS-V3 are not large but are visible 
in  Fig. 5, showing a sample scene, where we subtracted Sounder SIPS ATMS-V3 from 
the radiance calibration-based IDPS. We use channel 2, which is very sensitive to 
tropospheric fluctuations and surface changes. We see an obvious striping in this figure, 
which results from the different box averaging functions. As the box functions are 
slightly different, they can have different start and ending points. However, the impact is 
statistically random and on average we have only small differences << 0.1K.  

Figure 5: Brightness temperature differences of a random scene of Sounder SIPS 
ATMS-V3 and IDPS-radbased for channel 5 (granule 2, 2020/06/01) 

12 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 JPSS-1 ATMS Assessment Report for SNPP Sounder SIPS Level 1B 

2.3 Differences in Brightness Temperature and Radiance Calibration 

The key difference between IDPS TDR and SIPS ATMS is the calibration approach. 
SIPS ATMS is using brightness temperatures and all non-linear corrections are therefore 
in units of Kelvin. IDPS TDR switched to radiance units in 2017 and converted all 
coefficients to W/m2.  

The difference between the two approaches was discussed in the Assessment Report for 
Version 2 and is not repeated here in all detail. In summary, we processed this data with 
different configurations and got ”IDPS-like” results, which were compared to our 
Sounder SIPS ATMS data. A result for channel 2 is shown in Fig. 6. In this case, we 
compared the results between the new IDPS-calibration procedure (radiances) and old 
procedure (brightness temperature). The histograms show a slight difference between 
these two ways of calculation, but the differences from “no quadratic correction” are an 
order of magnitude smaller than  with quadratic correction. It indicates that the difference 
between radiance-based and brightness-temperature-based calculations is small until  
quadratic coefficients come into play. However the impact of quadratic coefficients on 
data from ATMS JPSS-1 is smaller than for ATMS SNPP data. Again, the impact is 
different for different channels ( see Addendum), but in general it is smaller for SNPP. 
The quadratic correction factors for ATMS JPSS-1 for NASA SIPS are directly taken 
from the calibration manual [2] and are in temperature units. However, we have no 
insight into the IDPS correction values and cannot determine the source of the 
differences. However, the smaller difference for JPSS-1 gives us confidence that both 
NASA SIPS and NOAA IDPS have small errors. 

Figure 6: Calculations with a modified IDPS code allows a comparison of the new 
calibration procedure (radiances) and old procedure (brightness temperature). The left 
panel shows the channel 2 differences between new and old procedure with quadratic 
correction, the right panel shows differences without correction (y-scale is enhanced in 
this plot). The remaining channels can be found in the Addendum. 

13 

 
 
 
 
  
 
 JPSS-1 ATMS Assessment Report for SNPP Sounder SIPS Level 1B 

2.4 Moon Angles 

Similar to V2, we use moon intrusion angles during cold space observations. All data that 
may be affected by moon intrusions are flagged erroneous. The error flagging is based on 
output from a standard package that is used for NASA geolocation calculations. Scenes 
viewing the moon are not discarded, but marked with an error flag. Also, similar to 
ATMS SNPP V2, the calibration is handled differently during lunar intrusion: the lunar 
intrusion is not a cold space view, so a reliable cold space count before the intrusion is 
used for calibration. This allows the processing of earth observations even during the 
intrusion.  We take into account that reliability decreases with the length of intrusion 
because the calibration coefficients become less accurate. Dependent on the time frame 
of the lunar intrusion, the data are flagged with increasing error values, which are 
described in the NASA NETCDF-file. The data are processed but always flagged with an 
error, which allows the users to decide how much they want to trust the data. 

For Sounder SIPS ATMS-V3, we still provide the same channel-dependent error flag as 
for Version 2. The  results are: 

•  Channel 1:  
•  Channels 3-9:   
•  Channels 10-16:  
•  Channels 17-22:  

7.4 degrees 
3.8 degrees 
3.2 degrees 
2.4 degrees 

The channels are clustered into four groups to keep the variation to a minimum. 
However, this also allows ,for example, the use of data from channels 17-22 when 
channel 1 is affected by lunar contamination. It also reduces the duration for higher 
channel numbers when lunar intrusion might affect the calibration calculation. Our plan 
is to verify and update this table for ATMS on SNPP and on JPSS-1 to ensure constant 
refinement to upcoming versions. 

2.5 Geolocation 

The geolocation (latitude and longitude) of Sounder SIPS ATMS-V3 is still 100% 
overlapping with IDPS. No changes were made for V3. 

The accuracy of the real geolocation in the telemetry can only be verified approximately 
due to the resolution of the footprints. However, for JPSS-1 there is another option: 
during the first months of testing, ATMS was put in a different mode for certain coastal 
overflights, called “stare mode observation”. The analysis of these coastline crossings 
gives highly accurate pointing information.  A preliminary investigation was shown in 
the assessment report for SNPP. The data are still being analyzed, but an update to the 
older version is provided below. 

14 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 JPSS-1 ATMS Assessment Report for SNPP Sounder SIPS Level 1B 

2.5.1 Geolocation Errors 

The stare mode of ATMS creates an array of very dense observations (~0.01 km) in the 
along-track direction. The stare mode was used during several coastal overpasses, where 
land fraction is changing from 0 to 1 (or vice versa). The broad spatial response functions 
of the ATMS antennas result in a ”smeared” observation of the coastal crossing. This 
results in a slow increase (or decrease) of observational counts. The observed change in 
counts from ATMS during the stare mode can therefore be compared to extremely well-
mapped land fractions. In an idealized case, the measured counts from ATMS will have a 
saddle point, when land fraction is changing from 0 to 1. 

The basic idea for this analysis is as follows.  The location of the saddle point should 
overlap with the change of land fraction. Any deviation of saddle point and land fraction 
= 0.5 would be deviations of the geolocation. We looked for this saddle point with a 
variety of mathematical derivations and smoothing processes, denoted with different 
annotations in Figure 6. The detailed approach will be described in a different document, 
which will be released at a later time. 

The averaged results are shown as black circles in Fig 7. By including all usable datasets 
and all mathematical approaches, we find very good agreement.  The current result (All) 
is a deviation of 1.8 km to the North-East (1.34 radians). The All results are slightly 
different from the first test, that is shown in the assessment report for SNPP. But they are 
still showing high accuracy. For channel 16, we have a distance of 1.00 km towards 
South-East (1.89 radians).  Compared to the footprint size these are both very accurate 
results.  We are developing correction factors for the instrument alignment to further 
increase the accuracy. 

Figure 7: Summary of the geolocation analysis for Channel 1 (left) and 16 (right). 

15 

 
 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 JPSS-1 ATMS Assessment Report for SNPP Sounder SIPS Level 1B 

3. Conclusion 

This document describes the assessment of JPSS-1 ATMS Level 1B Brightness 
Temperature V3 (SNDRJ1ATMSL1B V3).  That data sets is generated with an algorithm 
developed by SIPS at JPL. The two datasets for SNP and JPSS-1 use identical processing.  
This ensures data continuity for the JPSS-1 mission, but also further back to the 
beginning of SNPP ATMS.  This processing is different from IDPS ATMS TDR, since 
IDPS was changed in March 2017. According to the documentation, IDPS is converting 
everything in radiance units for the calibration procedure of TDR.  The NASA calibration 
of  L1B data is still calculated in temperature units, and the approach is applied to all 
datasets and all ATMS instruments.  

As a result, the NASA results are slightly different from the results from IDPS 
processing, which are available on NOAA CLASS.  These include small differences in 
observed temperatures, mostly < 0.1K, and mainly due to differences in the quadratic 
correction. SNDRJ1ATMSLIB V2  and IDPS both processed the data since launch in 
November 2017, but without any major changes to the software. The magnitude of 
differences between the two systems is therefore not changing, something we can 
confirm based on observations in February 2018 and November 2018. We will continue 
to monitor these variations throughout the mission.  

Version 3 was created because of 2 significant changes: The shelf temperature factors 
were corrected to create more accurate shelf temperature values. The changes are hereby 
small for SNPP and even smaller for JPSS-1. Nevertheless, the impact of the brightness 
temperatures was estimated. Statistics show, that the impact for SNPP is less than 0.003 
K and even less (8E-4 K) for JPSS-1.  

Another change was the correction of the sun glint angle for both systems. The correction 
resulted in more reasonable locations for the sung glint, especially with respect to the 
formation that SNPP and JPSS-1 create. The sun glint has no impact on the brightness 
temperatures and was therefore not further investigated. 

In general, this agreement suggests that SNDJ1ATMSL1B V3 is very accurate for current 
entire mission of JPSS-1. However, we want to point out that it is also backward 
compatible with data from ATMS SNPP( SNPPATMSL1B V3), as SNPPATMSL1B was 
reprocessed for the entire mission with a V3, going back to 2011.  Therefore, by 
combining SNPP and JPSS-1 data we have a seamless dataset since 2011, which is (at 
least for 2018) overlapping, and processed with the same code. We consider this a major 
advantage of ATMSL1B compared to other products, and this approach will be continued 
with other ATMS instruments. 

16
